speaker: interviewee
text: Where did we-- the first night, he brought me the kielbasa.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're back with the kielbasa. I'll break.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, slate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4, July 9, 1995. Interview with Maurice Myers, Flushing, New York, United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're back in the cell with the kielbasa.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the kielbasa--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're looking at you differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so the boys saw that I am somebody. I didn't do any dirty work or things like this. They needed, every morning, 10 boys to go out to work to bring in to the premises-- to the kitchen of the prison from-- across the street was Pawia Dzielna. The-- and in the basements, were laying a lot of vegetables-- beets, potatoes, cabbage-- which was prepared by the Jewish ghetto. And since it was empty already-- the ghetto was empty. So we had to bring in, on wheelers, the oldest stuff to the kitchen-- to our kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally, if you knew somebody, you could do it. Why? Because the Polacks went by. They didn't know that-- Jewish people or non-Jewish people. They know these are prisoners from Pawiak.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Polacks were, were walking. They threw a bread. They threw a pack of cigarettes. They threw away things, an apple, you know, for the prisoners. They did mitzvahs. Maybe if they would know they are Jews, they wouldn't do it. [LAUGHS] But they didn't know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, when we were in the 10 privileged workers, I was between them because I knew somebody. And the boys and the prison had kind of a respect for me. Respect? OK. They didn't know who I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know-- naturally, the third night came. Again, Oberscharfuhrer [? Muller ?] is again on duty. After the Appell, you come to the Schreibstube. And another kielbasa. twice he did it. And we became friends. Whenever he was on the floor in-- like, lunchtime, they give us the soup-- if he would be there, he would give me a double soup. He would give me, if he saw-- when we were standing in the line for soups. It happened that I got sick. I got a terrible cold, a grippe, with maybe 103 or 105 degrees. I-- it come-- one evening, [? Muller ?] come to the Appell. I am laying on the floor. I couldn't get up to the Appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And say, who is this? This is Majerczyk. He, he has temperature. Why doesn't he go to the-- to the-- what do you call it-- to the Krankenstube, for the sick people. Tomorrow he has to go to the Krankenstube.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next day, I got up. I had to go to the Krankenstube. But the next day, it was [NON-ENGLISH] already. And he saw that I am going through the corridor. The Ukrainian opened the door. He let me go through the corridor to the Krankenstube up a, a step, a, a, a few, like, 10, 15 steps up to the Krankenstube.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was standing at the door. And this [NON-ENGLISH], who is this? What are-- What are you do here? I say, I want to go to the Krankenstube.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, he hit me. He threw me down all the floors. I was on the floor downstairs. Anyway, with high temperature, I didn't know what he did. It didn't hurt me. I went back, and I laid down again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day, [? Muller ?] is there. Why aren't you at the Krankenstube? I said, I, I wanted to go, but [NON-ENGLISH] throw me down from all the step.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come with me! He takes me to the Krankenstube. There was a French-Jewish doctor prisoner-- Jewish or non-Jewish, I don't know, but a prisoner. French-- he speaks only French. He gives me, in those days, Ciba-- Cibalgina and Cibazol-- Ciba, this, this Swiss Ciba company, which we have today. This was the only thing against the cold, like against headache. He, he tells me, [SPEAKING FRENCH], three times a day. And you should drink a lot of water. Water-- I cannot drink tea. Water, we had. We had water. I drank water. And I-- and I-- he gave me 20 of these pills. And they put me back on my feet. I'm going out back to work. This is the story of 30 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living better in prison than people were living in the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so good in prison. I, I, I-- he didn't bring me any more kielbasa, just twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he gave me some soup. But I was treated as a human being over there. Because the worst is with the boys, with the other prisoners, you know what I mean? And I went out, naturally, back working right away. The 10th of March, after 30 days of being in prison, in the middle-- about-- in the morning-- we started to work at 8 o'clock, 8:00

speaker: interviewee
text: 00. About 10 o'clock, everybody back on the-- on the floor of the Pawiak. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We are with these things, bringing the vegetables. And we came back to the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] place. I see [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a German general, maybe three, four high officers-- Gestapo, Wehrmacht, about six, seven people-- and two Jewish navy blue uniform from the ghetto policeman, too. And I see the whole 350 people in five lanes. And on this side, they have picked about 20-some-- 20 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this general ask, who are they? The-- they told them, these are the Feldarbeiter, the people that are working for the-- for the prison. He asked me, what is your [GERMAN]? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I'm a Schlosser. This was the best answer. Schlosser means a mechanic-- a steel mechanic machinery. So I am number, maybe, 26 or 27. Three more, he stops the segregation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are 30 people standing this side, 350 people. He takes all the 350 people across the street to a building. The buildings in, in Warsaw was-- like, you went into a, a-- they had-- I don't know how to describe it. The building was around. You went in to a big door. And you had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you call it-- a big place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they took them all there. And we are standing, 30 people, the, the balance of the whole prison-- of the Jewish prison. And we hear some shots and shots and shots and shots-- nothing. Who knows what's going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the whole gang came back to us, the, the, the seven or eight Gestapo people with the Wehrmacht with them. And he had a speech to us. You 30 Jews are going to work for the Wehrmacht-- for the-- for the army! You are going to live through the war! You are going to be free people! You are being good [GERMAN]. Good food-- you are going to get good enough food to eat. You are no more prisoners! You are free people! It's good. And go down to the-- to your cell and take the coats, whatever you left there. Because it was wintertime. We-- I took my coat, my hat, my-- everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Ukrainian asked how many people-- how many soldiers-- how many Ukrainians should he give us for the [GERMAN], for the watchers, for security?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, they are free people! One is enough! You don't need so many. They are free people. They are going to work for us. I hear that-- beautiful. They took us in the afternoon out on this truck with one Ukrainian with a dog-- on the big truck, the 30 people. And we went out from the prison, from the Pawiak, across the street. And the-- and we saw blood running-- red blood running-- the whole street because they killed all the 350 prisoners. They made a massacre. And their blood was running in the street because they don't have-- they didn't have underground--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewers--

speaker: interviewee
text: --like we have in the United States. You could see it was running in the street. So this was the end of them and the beginning of us. We went to [NON-ENGLISH] 103. [NON-ENGLISH] 103 was the headquarters of the Jewish little ghetto-- little Judenrat. They had about 10, 15 policemen. And, and they were the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the story? The story was this. They asked for 50 Jewish workers from the ghetto. The Jewish workers had-- the ghetto had already guns. It was a few months before the uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, no Jew is leaving the, the ghetto-- the Warsaw Ghetto, the, the, the, the few streets what they had. A German officer wouldn't dare to go into a house in the ghetto because they would kill him.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was already an uprising going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: They fought--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was already--

speaker: interviewee
text: They would kill them. It-- they were ready. It-- the uprising was not done yet. They were ready. It was March. The uprising was, I think, a month or two months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Germans expected it, you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you said they wouldn't go in--

speaker: interviewee
text: So the, the-- and so the Jewish-- the Jewish Judenrat, they said-- they said, look, you have 350 Jews. They are the same Jews. They are not thieves or murderers. They are the same because they are Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you take the Jews from Pawiak? You have 50. Take the 50, have a few hundred. What's the difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe they paid them off or something, you know. Because they couldn't deliver them the Jews from the ghetto-- wouldn't listen to the Judenrat anymore. They were organized, and this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your survival of getting out of the prison helped the fact that there was an uprising that was going to happen at Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: The uprising--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they wouldn't help out the Judenrat, they had to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, if they were there--

speaker: interviewer
text: --come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we wouldn't-- and they would either kill us or send us to the-- take us to Auschwitz. How long would they keep us? A month, another few weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we are in [NON-ENGLISH] 103. They prepared, maybe, for us-- we were 30 boys-- about 50 breads. But these Polish breads, they're, they're, they're four pounders. I ate up from, from the beginning to the end, the whole bread. I'm telling you, a whole bread. I ate 2-kilogram bread, which is four pounds of bread, I ate. And everybody was so hungry. And the whole night, we were eating. They gave us coffee and bread. We had coffee and bread the whole night. We didn't-- naturally, we didn't sleep. But we were happy to be here, out of prison. In the morning, they take us with a truck to a, a, a railroad station, only to unload-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to unload the cars with Vichy water. Every case was about 24 bottles. And you had to carry this from the-- about, about 200 yards or 250 yards, to the railroad. This was a unit of the supply for the army. We were working for the army-- no more Gestapo, no more prison, but we were working for them. And two boys were left right away in the kitchen to cook for us. And we were carrying these cases, loading the, the railroad for the Eastern Front. Because they were in, in trouble already in Russia-- this water, drinking water. It came about 12:00, 12:00 noon. We started at 7:00, I think. 12:00 noon-- I see blood already on this side because-- from carrying. I'm not used to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I carried on this side. I carried on this side. I was-- you had to carry this running and, and carrying. It came-- until 4 o'clock, I think we were working, some 8 or 10 hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here was blood already. And here was blood. I say, good, I'm out of prison. But this is it. I'm going to die here. I told to one of the kitchen boys, listen, give me a break tomorrow. You see, it's all bloody. Let me-- I'll, I'll work in the kitchen tomorrow. Give me a day. And the next day, I'll let you back into the kitchen. Because I, I can really-- and he saw that this was all blood, and my-- and my skin was blood. And when they went out to work-- that was a big-- a big field-- a very big field. And as one soldier-- a Wehrmacht soldier was with a gun, walking back and forth, maybe a mile-long field. He was-- he didn't mind what we are doing if we are-- this was his business. He was just a security guard, you know. I took the wheeler with the potato stuff, which, which-- with the potato garbage stuff. And there was a big [GERMAN]-- in German, you call it-- a hill with this garbage. I went, I saw that when the Wehrmacht security guard was in front of me-- I was waiting-- and he started walking back, back, back, back, behind. I went up on this hill with my-- with my wheeler, up. And it was barbed wire. I jumped over the barbed wire, over the thing. And I was outside already in Warsaw, in Zoliborz. The section is called Zoliborz. Now I was a free man-- half-free man, not a whole free man. But I was again out of prison. And it was exactly 30 days. And where do I-- I meet right away a Polack, a, a small Polack, a, a young boy, maybe 12 years old. This was-- this was the, the bad section from Warsaw, you know. Fine people didn't live in there. They lived in these shanties over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, oh, my my-- my dad is a thief. You know? My dad is-- my mother is also. You, you are a Jew. You just run out from the ghetto. I say, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we save Jews for $100. I say, listen. I have a sister in the ghetto, in Warsaw Ghetto. I'll give you $100. Let's make a date tomorrow morning. I'll meet you. I had to go to Inflancka. I didn't know how to go to Inflancka. And with him, when I was working, I was safe-- with a Polack, you know. And, and I had a piece of cheese to give him, a piece of cheese which I took from the kitchen. That's all I have to give you. But tomorrow, I'm going to give you $100. And you will bring out my, my sister. I will tell you where and what-- I will give it to you. But I-- take me now to Inflancka 30. My sister was Inflancka 1, but I wouldn't reveal him the address.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you know where your sister was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you know where your sister was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well I was in touch with my sister. I brought her to Warsaw. Oh, I didn't tell you this. Later, I brought her-- the sames [NON-ENGLISH], the ones who brought me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Brought her.

speaker: interviewee
text: --made her also papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --made also papers. And they brought her also.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you ran into this little boy, did you still have your papers? Your false papers-- or they were long gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: He recognized me right away. I had nothing anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took away everything from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had nothing. It-- papers didn't mean nothing. The, the Polacks-- recognizes you right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you met up with your sister again.

speaker: interviewee
text: The way how he took me to number 30. I said, OK, goodbye. I'll meet you here tomorrow at 9 o'clock, remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went up to the attic, waiting there for half an hour. When I went down, I see clean. I went till number 1. I didn't know how to go to Inflancka Street, you know? And so he took me there. And I went up to my sister. My sister says, I know you're going to be free. How do I know? How do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, I was at a fortune teller. And she told me that I'm going to see again my brother, who's in trouble now. But you're going to see him again. Anyway, this woman could hold me only a couple of days because two Jews in a home was too much-- was a crowd already. She was kind enough to keep me. And then they found me places-- here I was a week, was no good. Here I was a few days, was no good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, they found me a place-- Mickiewicza 34, in Zoliborz, in Warsaw, on the west side of the-- of the Wisla, of the big river, with a very fine family. He was director of [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] means? Like Metropolitan Life Insurance.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was a government-- you belonged to the government. There was no private insurance in Poland. It was a government. And he was the director. Naturally, when the Germans came, no more director, no more [NON-ENGLISH], no more insurance. He didn't make a living. He had a beautiful apartment-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two bedrooms, a beautiful living room, a, a, a den, a kitchen, and a beautiful balcony on the fourth floor. Also, a month-- 3,000 zloty a month. I still had money. My sister hold the bundle because I was in prison. My-- but still I had my shoes, with two pieces of 20 gold pieces, you know. And I still had my buckles. Sorry I didn't bring it with me to-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: --show it to you. I made up a golden heavy [LAUGHS] buckle-- And you-- and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --made out of gold.

speaker: interviewee
text: --were able to stay with this man--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it. I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it!

speaker: interviewer
text: You still have it? Were you able to stay with this man throughout the duration of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then there's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That is another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, while you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the big story.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were with him, though, did you observe the uprising in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I was with him, I was in a place where the-- they didn't know that I am Jewish because if people-- if they didn't know that I'm Jewish, was danger. Why? Because any time, they could recognize me, and they could take me to the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was there one evening in a certain place and a [NON-ENGLISH] came in-- a Polish woman-- my name was Stefan. Oh, [NON-ENGLISH], let's go on the roof. We'll see how the Jews are burning there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I had to go up with all the other people on the roof. And we saw the fire of every building in the-- in the-- in the ghetto. But this was a side story. This wasn't my main story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you got to this final place that you were at?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got to this Mickiewicza 34 in Warsaw. And they were very elegant people. Like, she had to have a, a, a girl-- a cleaning girl in the house-- a living-up maid, she had to have. Because this is how she was used. But since her husband couldn't afford it, they could-- they took in a Jew. And the Jew paid. And they could have a maid, too. At 5 o'clock was tea. 12 o'clock to the second was lunch. 9 o'clock was breakfast. 6 o'clock was-- I think, 7 o'clock was dinner, to the minute. It was a very elegant house. This was the Polish aristocracy. In the afternoon, once a week, he went to play bridge. He went to play bridge-- very elegant people. They had a daughter and a son-in-law, which lost a leg, which was in the hospital-- an officer, a doctor, which was still in the hospital. And his uniforms were hanging in my bedroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yet you had to leave their place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yet you left them also. Why did-- why did you leave their--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I'll tell you the story. And during the day, I, I put on my uniform, walking around in a Polish officer's uniform. So whoever-- the, the girl who was working for us-- saw that I am a part of the family, a cousin with the uniform. I was a-- and one day, they went away the-- to play bridge. And this girl was alone. And she came into my room and told me-- this woman, not a girl, a woman, middle-aged woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she saw-- oh, I didn't go out. And I was-- she asked, why doesn't he go out in the street? Oh, because he has TB. I have-- I'm supposed to have TB, that I cannot go out. And naturally, if you don't go out, you are pale after the prison. I was pale enough and sick-- looked sick enough. So she came to my room. And she said, I'm so lonely. You know, I am a teacher. I took this job because I was from Poznan, displaced, and I couldn't get a teaching job. I am a teacher of languages. Would you want-- pick up a language, French or English? I would like to teach you. You have so much time, you sit in the house. I say, you are an English teacher? And I have been in England, she said. I said, you are an English teacher? And you learn languages, and you are a housemaid, peeling potatoes and cooking? So she says, you know, you have-- the nose is a little bit too long, the ears are too big. You are not blond. You don't have blue eyes. I said-- and her name was Anna. I said, Anna, you are Jewish? So she says-- she says, well-- I say, Anna, I am Jewish, too. [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But [CRYING] this scene [SOBBING] was unbelievable. She started hugging me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maurice, she says-- Stefan, you are in the wrong place. This is a dangerous room where you are living. I have to find you a better place. I am in contact with the Polish underground. I will find you a better place. It's not good here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And naturally, we started English-- a little bit of English. The-- and then she told me who I was. She was-- her name-- Anna [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was the-- one of the richest textile manufacturers in Poland, Jewish people. The first-- which they make rayons. In Poland, rayon came out before the war. They were the ones-- the first factory of rayons, employing thousands of people. The only son he has, [NON-ENGLISH], who was killed-- went right away. And she was the only wife and heiress. Her vacation was in French or in England before the war, I want you to know. And she was only a professor of languages in the university. This was-- she was shining my shoes in the morning once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she help you find a new place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- one day-- I was, every day, dressed, ready to run. In the morning, I didn't wear my, my house slippers, but my good shoes, always ready. Because I never know if I'm going to live through the day. Either-- because I was walking and making noise with the shoe every day, somebody sent the Gestapo up. Either this [NON-ENGLISH] found out where I am, or a super from the house-- from the building, something-- I cannot tell you how.

speaker: interviewee
text: Banging at the door, but banging on a big scale-- I see, this is it again. This time, no more [? Muller, ?] no more Gestapo, no more thing. I'm dead now. What do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a split second-- the door was locked. I ran in to the-- to the bathroom. The bathroom had a window, a small window which you could go through to the balcony. I locked the bathroom. I went-- it took seconds. I went out through the balcony and crossed over the iron gate from the balcony on the fourth floor. And I hanged myself up-- on the fingers, I hanged myself. And I jumped into the third-floor balcony. I was in the third-floor balcony on somebody's apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman said, oh, who are you? I am afraid-- I am afraid Gestapo's in this house. You see, downstairs there's the Gestapo car.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, OK. I know the Gestapo is in the house. Take me to the door. Because I didn't know her apartment. She took-- oh, yes, good. Leave right away because I'm scared. She took me to the door, and I went downstairs. Gestapo car was there standing. The Gestapo was up in my room. And I, again, disappeared. I didn't have where to go too much. They had a daughter. One night I was with the daughter. And most of the nights I was sleeping in attics-- in, in Warsaw in attics. Because my sister's boss couldn't take me in-- her, her woman couldn't take me in because one Jew is enough. Two Jews is a already, naturally. Till this Anna [NON-ENGLISH] got in touch with my sister, and she gave me a place in Grodzisk, [BACKROUND NOISE] which is 15 or 17 miles west of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is it?
